coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a new hyperbranched polymer for the preparation of a polymer dot as a supercapacitor with enhanced electrochemical performance

coredata.dc:description: This study presents a facile method to fabricate a nanocomposite of a new polymer dot wrapped in cobalt oxide as a high-performance supercapacitor. Therefore, a new highly branched polymer of promising materials in electrochemical applications was synthesized and then simultaneously converted into a polymer dot and combined with cobalt oxide. The nanocomposites were prepared at different temperatures, and the optimal temperature for the preparation of a high-performance electroactive material was determined. The nanocomposite prepared at 180 °C (CoO-PDHPB-180) was selected as an appropriate electroactive material. This electrode showed superior electrochemical behavior with a specific capacitance of 664 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Also, a symmetric device was prepared from CoO-PDHPB-180 that showed a specific capacity of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, a maximum energy density of 62.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of up to 754 W kg−1. In addition, the LED lamp was lighted using the symmetric device, which confirmed the results obtained from the electrochemical analysis. The good electrochemical activity of the CoO-PDHPB-180 electrode was ascribed to a large number of functional groups of the hyperbranched polymer-derived polymer dot, which led to increased synergistic effects between the components.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a new hyperbranched polymer for the preparation of a polymer dot as a supercapacitor with enhanced electrochemical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents a facile method to fabricate a nanocomposite of a new polymer dot wrapped in cobalt oxide as a high-performance supercapacitor. Therefore, a new highly branched polymer of promising materials in electrochemical applications was synthesized and then simultaneously converted into a polymer dot and combined with cobalt oxide. The nanocomposites were prepared at different temperatures, and the optimal temperature for the preparation of a high-performance electroactive material was determined. The nanocomposite prepared at 180 °C (CoO-PDHPB-180) was selected as an appropriate electroactive material. This electrode showed superior electrochemical behavior with a specific capacitance of 664 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Also, a symmetric device was prepared from CoO-PDHPB-180 that showed a specific capacity of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, a maximum energy density of 62.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of up to 754 W kg−1. In addition, the LED lamp was lighted using the symmetric device, which confirmed the results obtained from the electrochemical analysis. The good electrochemical activity of the CoO-PDHPB-180 electrode was ascribed to a large number of functional groups of the hyperbranched polymer-derived polymer dot, which led to increased synergistic effects between the components.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy storage in the form of electrical energy is one of the most important challenges facing human societies, which is progressing towards reducing the use of fossil fuels [1,2]. In this regard, electric energy storage is the challenge facing societies. Effective technologies used for energy storage are solar cells, batteries, and supercapacitors devices [3–5]. Supercapacitors have attracted more attention than other energy storage devices due to desirable advantages such as high-power density, longer life cycle, and low cost [6–9]. However, the low energy density of supercapacitors has be remained a challenge. In early researches, metal oxides were used in the construction of supercapacitors, but slight electrical conductivity, out of control volumetric expansion, and low ion diffusion in the solid phase limited their use in supercapacitors [10,11]. Therefore, modifying and improving the electrochemical properties of metal oxides through their composites with other materials such as conductive polymers, carbon materials and quantum dots has attracted the attention of scientists [12–14]. When quantum dots are composited with metal oxides, due to the synergistic effect, the electrochemical properties of the final composite are improved compared to alone quantum dots and metal oxide alone [15]. For example,.M. Vandana and et.al prepared a composite based on tin oxide quantum dots/graphene oxide/polypyrrole for high energy storage applications [16]. R. Suresh Babu and et.al reported the preparation of a high energy and power density supercapacitor from polythiophene and manganese cobalt oxide as an asymmetric supercapacitor [17]. Polymer dots are a group of quantum dots that have both the properties of quantum dots and the properties of polymers [18]. Polymer dots in their structure have sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and functional groups that facilitate the mobility of ions and provide additional places for charge storage. Polymer dots have many functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, amine on their surface, which improves the penetration of the electrolyte and, as a result, improves the electrochemical properties [19,20]. Polymer dots are synthesized in different ways. One of the easy methods for the preparation of polymer dots is synthesis from polymers [18,21].

Hyperbranched polymers are a group of polymers with excellent properties such as branching structure, three-dimensional structure, easy synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and many internal or surface functional groups [22,23]. The branched structure of the hyperbranched polymer led to an increase in the d-spacing of formed quantum dot stacks, which facilitate access to sites for charge storage, and inhibit accumulation [24,25]. Also, owning to high number of functional groups led to increasing of the mobility of ions and provides additional places for charge storage [26–28]. Hence, these polymers can be a unique material for preparing polymer dots with many functional groups. Despite to best quality of the hyperbranched polymer, there are only a few hyperbranched polymers that have been used in the preparation of polymer quantum dots for energy storage applications [29]. Therefore, in the present study, a new hyperbranched was designed and synthesized from promising materials in energy storage devises. The prepared hyperbranched polymer was converted to polymer dot and simultaneously composited with CoO in one-step in several temperatures to obtain a high-performance CoO-derived nanocomposite. The obtained nanocomposites were fully characterized and their electrochemical properties were evaluated.

Glutamic acid, Glucose, Melamine, and Propan-1-ol, and N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium hydroxide ((KOH)), Cobalt (II) acetate, Dimethylformamide (DMF), Ethanol, Methanol, Potassium chloride (KCl), and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Merck.

For studying of the chemical structure of the synthesized compounds the FT-IR and 1HNMR spectroscopy were performed by Bruker TENSOR II FTIR spectrometer and Bruker Avance III™ HD 400 MHz, respectively. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM TESCAN/MIRA3) was used to recording the pictures of morphology of the compounds. The mapping and elemental analyses of the compounds were determined using an energy dispersive X-ray analysis XRD instrument (Shimadzu /EDX-8000). Absorbance spectra were collected by Shimadzu UV-Vis spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were recorded using a Bruker /D8 ADVANCE instrument for investigation of the crystalline structure of the prepared compounds. A FEI /TEC9G20 High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) was employed to study detailed structural information of samples. The photoluminescence spectra of the samples were collected by a Cary Eclipse photoluminescent spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hyperbranched poly glutamic- melamine- glucamide (HBPGMG )
ce:section.ce:para: The HBPGMG copolymer was synthesized in one-step through polycondensation method. In brief, the glutamic acid (2 g, 100 mmol), glucose (2.28 g, 13.9 mmol), melamine (0.78 g, 6.18), 15 mL NMP, and 15 mL propan-1-ol were condensed under stirring for 48 h at 100 ℃. Then, the precipitated solid brown product was separated by centrifuge, washed by NMP/ethanol several times, and dried at 50°C. The efficiency of the obtained brown hyperbranched polymer was 92 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cobalt oxide- polymer dot nanocomposite based on HBPGMG (CoO-PDHBP)
ce:section.ce:para: The CoO-PDHBP nanocomposite was prepared by solvothermal one-step method. 0.2 g of the HBPGMG hyperbranched copolymer, 0.8 g of cobalt acetate (3.21 mmol) were dissolved in 12 mL of DMF and 12 mL ethanol, poured in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and heated at 150, 180, 200 °C in an oven. After 48 h, the obtained solid product was isolated using a centrifuge, washed three times with ethanol, and dried at room temperature in the dark.

For recording photoluminescence spectra of the samples, each of the prepared nanocomposites was dispersed in deionized water. Quinine sulfate was considered as a reference (QY= 0.54). The QY of nanocomposites was calculated by Eq. 1. The value of ŋ was considered 1.33 for water (as the solvent) [30,31].

To investigate the electrochemical behavior of active materials in the three-electrode system, graphite, Ag/AgCl, and graphite electrodes were used as working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. Also, a 2 M KOH solution was used as an electrolyte. To prepare the working electrode of the active material, (PVDF) polyvinylidene fluoride adhesive and activated carbon with a specific ratio (8:1:1) were dissolved in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 minutes. The resulting solution was added to the surface of the graphite electrode (1 * 1 cm) and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h. The specific capacitance was calculated using the following formulas:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of HBPGMG and CoO-PDHBP nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
The copolymer HBPGMG was obtained from three monomers of melamine, glutamic acid, and glucose using the polycondensation method in one-step (
Scheme 1). Melamine is an A3-type monomer, glutamic acid is an AB2-type monomer, and glucose controls the size of polymer nanoparticles. Melamine is widely used in the preparation of electroactive materials [36–38]. Due to the large number of nitrogen atoms in the structure of melamine, it could facilitate ion mobility of ions and provides additional places for charge storage. Also, the glutamic acid and glucose owning to COOH and NH2 functional groups could facilitate the penetration of electrolytes and improve the electrochemical properties [39]. In the second step, a polymer dot was synthesized from HBPGMG under solvothermal conditions at three different temperatures (150, 180, and 200 ℃), simultaneously composited with the oxidized cobalt, and creates the CoO-PDHBP-150–200 nanocomposites (Scheme 1).

The synthesized hyperbranched polymer has a large number of surface and internal functional groups, so the synthesis of a polymer dot from this leads to a polymer dot with an increasingly large number of NH2, OH, and CO2H functional groups, which act as high-performance materials in energy storage devices. Also, among the various supercapacitor electrode materials, transition metal oxides offer high electronegativity, rich redox reactions, environmental friendliness, and excellent electrochemical performance. Furthermore, the cobalt oxides have received significant interest in recent years because of their low cost, non-toxic, easy synthesis, and environmentally friendly nature. The cobalt oxides have high capacitance [40,41]. These have the ability to interact with the ions of the electrolyte at the surface as well as through the bulk of the material [42].

The structure of the prepared hyperbranched polymer was identified by 1H NMR ( Fig. 1a). Since HBPGMG is a copolymer containing amide bonds between the CO=OH groups of glutamic acid and the NH2 groups of melamine and glutamic acid (Fig. 1a), the environments of the formed amid groups are different and the hydrogen of amides appeared at different frequencies (7.9–8.0 ppm). The chemical change observed at δ= 6.0 is related to unreacted NH2 groups of melamine [43,44]. The CH2 groups of glutamic acid were seen in 1.25 and 2.5 ppm. The α'¬-CH hydrogens of glutamic acid blocks have appeared in the range of 3.3–3.5 ppm. Also, the NH peak of glutamic acid reacted with glucose was appeared in this area [45]. Extensive chemical shifts in the region of 2.75–2.91 ppm were ascribed to groups of -CH assigned to the building blocks of glucose. The chemical shifts at 3.22 and 3.05 ppm were attributed to the CH2 groups adjacent to the NH and OH groups of glucose units [46,47].

FT-IR spectra of the prepared compounds were recorded for more investigations of the chemical structures (Fig. 1b). In the spectrum of the HBPGMG, the peak at 1645 cm−1 was related to CO stretching vibration of amide functional groups, which conforms the formation of hyperbranched poly amid from applied materials. The peaks at 1431 cm−1 and 1544 cm−1 ascribed to bending vibration of CN and NH2 groups of melamine The relatively strong peak at 3469 cm−1, 3419 cm−1 and 3329 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of NH2 and NH groups, respectively. The peak at 3100 cm−1 was attributed to the hydrogen bonded to melamine aromatic rings. In the spectrum of the CoO-PDHBP-150 nanocomposite, the broad peak appearing in the range of 2700–3600 cm−1 is related to the OH of carboxylic acid groups on the formed polymer dot. Also, the weak bands observed at 2750 cm−1 and 2858 cm−1 are related to aldehyde groups in polymer dot [48]. The strong peak observed in the range of 1650–1720 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of the CO of amid, aldehyde, and carboxylic acid groups in the structure of polymer and polymer dot. In the spectra of the CoO-PDHBP-180 and CoO-PDHBP-200, the peaks observed at 2700–3600, 2750 and 2858 cm−1 were more intense than the CoO-PDHBP-150, indicating the formation of more polymer dots at 180, 200 ℃ than at 150 ℃. The broad peak observed at 640–900 cm−1 in the spectra of the CoO-PDHBP-180 and CoO-PDHBP-200, ascribed to out-of-plane bending of C-H of benzene rings in the formed polymer dot, which over-lapped with Co-O peaks at 670 cm−1 [49,50].

The phase structures of the nanocomposites were studied by XRD analysis. As seen in Fig. 1c, XRD spectra of the CoO-PDHBP-150, CoO-PDHBP-180, and CoO-PDHBP-200 nanocomposites show the amorphous. In XRD spectra of the CoO-PDHBP-150, CoO-PDHBP-180, and CoO-PDHBP-200 nanocomposites, a peak has appeared at 2θ of 11.7, 12.7, and 11.5, respectively, which similar to the (001) plane of the graphene oxide diffraction peak (polymer dots have a structure similar to graphene oxide). The graphene oxide peak usually observes at 2θ of 10.2, so the change in 2θ of the nanocomposites from 10.2 can be ascribed to the presence of the hyperbranched polymer with a high value of functional groups bonded to the edge of the crystalline plates, which leads to an increase the distance between the layer and increasing 2θ value [51]. Also, the branched structure of the polymer leads to an increase in the d-spacing of formed quantum dot stacks, which inhibit accumulation and facilitate access to sites for charge storage [52,53]. Meanwhile, the intensity of the peak in each nanocomposite was different, showing the amount of formed polymer dot in each nanocomposite. When the temperature is 150 ○C, polymer dot is formed in low amount with some impurity of CoO and HBPGMG. When the temperature was increasing to 180 ○C, PDHBP is formed more and more and the peak intensity of 2θ = 12.7 increase [54]. Also, when the reaction temperature is extended to 200, the decomposition of functional groups of the hyperbranched polymer is increased, so the 2θ was decreased. The best conditions are determined to obtain CoO-PDHBP at a temperature of 180 ○C. Also, in the XRD spectra of the nanocomposites, a diffraction peak was located at 2θ= 25.6, which ascribed to the quantum dots corresponds to the (002) plane. The peaks at 25.6 and 12.6 proved the synthesis of the PDHBP from the HBPGMG hyperbranched polymer. The peaks observed in 36.54 ͦ, 42.44 ͦ, 61.53 ͦ, 73.82 ͦ, and 77.66 ͦ are related to cobalt oxide that corresponds to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes, respectively, that verifies the existence of cobalt oxide in the structure of nanocomposites. Meanwhile, the TGA analysis was carried out for more investigation (Fig. S1 (Supplementary Information)).

Fig. S1 shows the TGA analysis for the CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 nanocomposites at 0–700°C under nitrogen gas. In the CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 nanocomposites at 0–100 °C, the weight decreased by 5 %, 4 %, and 2 %, respectively, which was related to surface evaporation. In the nanocomposite, 18 % of the weight was decreased at 100–210, related to the removal of hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups of the PDHPB. Then, 24.9 % of the weight decreased at 280–700 °C due to the removal of functional groups on the surface of the PDHPB polymer dot (such as amine, carboxyl, and hydroxyl) and the removal of free chains. Meanwhile, due to the presence of the more HBPGMG hyperbranched polymer in the structure of CoO-PDHPB-150 and CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposites, these nanocomposites were more weight loss than the CoO-PDHPB-200 nanocomposite [55].

The constituent elements and elemental distribution of the obtained polymer and nanocomposites were studied using mapping and EDX analyses (Fig. S2). The obtained results showed the presence of N, O, and C elements in the structure of the HBPGMG and N, O, C, and Co in the structure of the nanocomposites. The morphology of the prepared compounds were studied using FE-SEM, TEM, and HR-TEM. As seen from Fig. S3a, SEM image shows three dimentional shape with a size of about 50–80 nm for HBPGMG polymer, that prove synthesis of hyperbranched polymer from melamine, glutamic acid, and glucose. Fig. S3b-d show the SEM images of the prepared nanocomposites at different temperatures (150, 180, and 200 ○C). The CoO-PDHBP-150 nanocomposite showed an incomplete and shapeless structure, which indicates the incomplete composition of formed PDHBP polymer dot with cobalt oxide (Fig. S3b). According to the SEM and TEM images, it could be diagnos that the CoO-PDHBP-180 and CoO-PDHBP-200 nanocomposotes have a spherical woven shape, which indicate the complete composition of formed PDHBP polymer dot with cobalt oxide at 180 and 200 ℃ (Fig. S3c-d). The woven structure of the nanocomposites could increase the electrochemical activity due to the faster transfer of electrons and ions [56]. When the temperature is 150 ○C, CoO-PDHBP is forms with some impurity of CoO and HBPGMG. The impurity disappeared when the temperature was increased to 180–200 ○C, and the CoO-PDHBP nanocomposite form in a woven spherical shape. These results are completely consistent with the results obtained from the XRD studies.

Fig. S 2a and b show the TEM of the CoO-PDHBP-180, according to this image, particle size of the CoO-PDHBP −180 nanocomposite is 400 nm. Fig. S 2c and d show the HR-TEM images of the CoO-PDHBP −180 with a magnification of 10 nm. This image showed a lattice margin at 0.28 nm, which is related to the plate (111) of CoO. Notably, honeycomb lattices with hybrid armchair-zigzag edges of a 0.33 nm graphene dot in the structure of nanocomposite were clearly observed which is related to the formation of highly crystalline polymer dot from HBPGMG [57,58]. The obtained data are completely consistent with the XRD results. The SAED image of the CoO-PDHBP −180 showed a polycrystalline structure ( Fig. 2e).

In the UV–vis spectra of the PDHBP and CoO-PDHBP-150-200, a peak is obtained at 265 nm relative to the π-π* transitions of the C═C aromatic bonds of the sp2 hybridization domains (Fig. S4a). Also, an absorption shoulder related to the n-π* transition of the C-N bonds on the surface of the PDHBP, was detect in the region of 290–320 nm. The PDHBP and CoO-PDHBP-150–180 were excited at 265 nm (Fig. S4b). The compounds showed two fluorescence emissions in the visible range. The photoluminescent properties of the compounds are related to the coupled combination of the localized electron-hole couple in sp2 clusters in the QD. Also, two fluorescence emission properties are attributed to interaction between the NH2, CO2H, OH functional groups, and CoO in the structure of the compounds [59]. It can be seen that the emission peaks gradually move into the long wavelength area. Also, in the fluorescence spectrum of compound CoO-PDHBP-200, the functional groups of the hyperbranched polymer are destroyed with increasing temperature, so interaction between Co and the polymer was decreased, leading to one fluorescence absorption. The QY of the CoO-PDHBP-150–200 were calculated at values of 45.4 %, 53.3 %, and 21.9 %, respectively (quinine sulfate was considered as a standard with QY of 54 %).

To investigate the supercapacitor performance of nanocomposites (CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200) at different temperatures based on polymer dots derived from the new hyperbranched polymer, the CV, GCD, and EIS analyses were performed in 2 M KOH electrolyte solution. Fig. 3a shows the CV curves of nanocomposites (CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200) at the scan rate of 50 mV/s in the potential window of −0.2–0.5 V. The general shape of the CV curves confirms the pseudocapacitors' behavior of nanocomposites, due to the reversible redox reaction of functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen on the surface of polymer dots. In other words, the nitrogen groups of the functional groups of the surface of the polymer dot increase the absorption of electrolyte ions and act as electron donors due to their high electronegativity. Comparison of CV curves of nanocomposites showed the highest specific capacitance and surface area for CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite. Functional groups of OH, COOH, and NH on the surface of polymer dots increase the speed of ion transfer and the specific capacitance [60]. The synergistic effect between cobalt oxide and the polymer dot increases with the increase of the amount of OH, COOH, and NH functional groups on the surface of the polymer dot. According to the results of chemical analysis, the CoO-PDHPB-150 nanocomposite has fewer OH, COOH, and NH functional groups than the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite at the surface of the polymer dot. Therefore, the specific capacitance of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite is higher than that of the CoO-PDHPB-150 nanocomposite. In the CoO-PDHPB-200 nanocomposite, the polymer was destroyed at 200°C, and the surface functional groups OH, COOH, and NH were reduced, which decreased the specific capacitance. Fig. 3b, c, and d show the cyclic voltammetry of CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 nanocomposites at different scan rates from 10 to 100 mV/s in the potential window of −0.2–0.5 V. The good reversibility of the nanocomposites caused the shape of the CV curves to remain stable. In the curves of Fig. 3b, c, and d, two redox peaks were related to Faradaic redox reactions of Co2+/Co3+ redox pairs, which confirm the pseudocapacitance process of nanocomposite electrodes. However, the intensity of these peaks is low in the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite electrode, which confirms the improvement of supercapacitance behavior [21,61]. The specific capacitances of CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 electrode materials were calculated about 285 F g−1, 714 F g−1, and 454 F g−1 at 50 mV/s, respectively. An important factor in supercapacitors is cyclic stability. In Fig. 4e, the cyclic stability of CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 electrode materials was calculated at 100 mv/s about 90 %, 95 %, and 92 %, respectively. Fig. 3g shows the current density of anode and cathode peaks (Fig. 3f) at different scan rates. The linear relationship between the scan rate and the current density of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite confirmed its excellent reversibility and ideal charge transfer process. The process was also kinetically rapid diffusion controlled [62,63].

Fig. 4a shows the GCD diagram of CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 electrode materials at a current density of 1 A g−1 in −0.2–0.5 V. The nonlinear shape of the GCD curves confirms the pseudocapacitance behavior caused by Faradaic redox reactions (Co2+/Co3+) in all nanocomposites, which is consistent with the results CV. The specific capacitance of CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 electrode materials were calculated to be about 282 F g−1, 664 F g−1, and 354 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Fig.s 4b, c, and d show GCD diagrams of CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 electrode materials at different current densities. In all nanocomposites, the current density increases with the increase of specific capacitance. Fig. 4e shows the graph of specific capacitance calculated by GCD analysis at different current densities to compare the energy storage capability of nanocomposites. As the current density increases, the specific capacitance of the electrode material decreases due to the insufficient involvement of the electrode material in the redox reaction. The specific capacitances of CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite at the current density of 1, 2.5, and 5 A g−1 were calculated to be about 664, 642, and 607 F g−1, respectively. The CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite maintains 83 % of its initial capacitance with increasing current density, which indicates good energy storage [64].

To investigate the practical application of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite, the symmetric device of CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite was made by a pair copper current collector, separator, and 6 M KOH electrolyte. Then the electrochemical analyses of CV, GCD, and EIS were investigated. Fig. 5a shows the CV diagram of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite device in the potential window of 0–1.5 V at various scan rates of 10–100 mV/s. The appearance shape of the CV diagram confirmed the pseudocapacitance behavior of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite device. Also, the shape of the curve is stable with scan rate increases, which shows excellent reversibility and high-speed performance. The specific capacitance of the nanocomposite device by cyclic voltammetry is to be about 175 F g−1 at 50 mV/s. Fig. 5b shows the GCD diagram of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite at the current density of 1–5 A g−1 in the potential window of 0–1.5 V. The specific capacitance of the symmetric device was calculated using the GCD diagram to be about 200 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. According to the recent study, it has been demonstrated that the capacity increased with the increase of the amount of oxygen functional groups, highlighting the significant effects of oxygen-containing functional groups on high storage performance and enhanced ionic conductivity [65–68]. Fig. 5c shows the speed capability of the device based on the total mass of the cathode and anode (the specific capacitance of the symmetric device in different current densities). The specific capacitance of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite device was calculated to be about 200, 183, 166, and 148 F g−1 in the current density of 1, 2.5, 5, and 10 A g−1. The symmetric device maintained 74 % of its initial capacitance by increasing the current density. Fig. 5d shows the cyclic stability of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite device to be about 91 % up to 1000 cycles [69].

Energy density and power density are important indicators in evaluating the performance of supercapacitors, so the plot of energy density vs. power density (Ragone plot) of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite device was investigated in Fig. 6a. The results show the supercapacitor has a maximum energy density of 62.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 754 W kg−1. In addition, the supercapacitor can maintain an energy density of 45.33 Wh kg−1 at a higher power density [70,71]. Fig. 6b shows the Nyquist diagram of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite device. The CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite device due to good charge transfer kinetics has a vertical line and a semicircle at the low frequency and high frequency, respectively. The proposed equivalent circuit for impedance data processing includes RS, RCT, SPE-T, and SPE-P elements, where RS and RCT represent equivalent series and charge transfer resistance, respectively. CPE- T is related to the non-ideal behavior of the nanocomposite that absorbs the electrolyte solution. CPE-P is a unit of dimensionless numbers, and the closer it is to 1 indicates the ideal behavior of the supercapacitor [21,72]. The results of impedance data processing according to the equivalent circuit are reported in Table 1.
Finally, the symmetric device of the CoO-PDHPB-180 nanocomposite turned on the white LED lamp.

Here, a novel hyperbranched copolymer (HBP) was designed and synthesized to achieve promising materials for advanced energy storage systems. The HBP was transformed into a polymer dot and combined with CoO at different temperatures at the same time. The prepared polymer dot from the hyperbranched polymer as a carbon material with chains of polymer and large amounts of functional group can improve the performance of supercapacitors. The supercapacitor performance of prepared nanocomposites at different temperatures was fully studied in the three-electrode device and the two-electrode device. In the studies of the three-electrode system, the specific capacitance of CoO-PDHPB-150, CoO-PDHPB-180, and CoO-PDHPB-200 nanocomposites were 282 F g−1, 664 F g−1, and 354 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, respectively. The increase of specific capacitance at 180 °C ascribed to large number of OH, COOH, and NH functional groups of the hyperbranched polymer, which increase the speed of ion transfer. Meanwhile, the reduction of the specific capacitance at 200 °C is due to many functional groups being destroyed. The symmetric device of nanocomposite was prepared to investigate its practical application using a copper current collector and 6 M electrolyte solution, and then electrochemical analyses were investigated for it. The symmetric device of nanocomposite showed a specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Also, the symmetric device showed a maximum energy density of 62.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 754 W kg−1. In this study, it can be concluded that nanocomposites prepared from the polymer dots of the hyperbranched polymer have high ion transfer rates and conductivity and are an ideal candidate for making supercapacitors.
